title: Finn, the Giant, and the Minster of Lund
summary: A giant named Finn builds Lund Cathedral in exchange for Saint Lawrence's eyes, but the saint overhears Finn's name in a lullaby and Finn turns to stone in the crypt. This world-famous master-builder legend is connected to several European cathedrals; Finn's petrified form can still be seen grasping a pillar in the crypt of Lund Cathedral.
full_text:
FINN, THE GIANT, AND THE MINSTER OF LUND
        IV
moral: Knowledge is power; a name held in secret can overcome even the strongest foe.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.III: Finn, the Giant, and the Minster of Lund. Retold by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund from variants in his collection. Connected to the world-famous giant master-builder legend, also associated with Nidaros Cathedral in Trondheim.

title: The Specter in Fjelkinge
summary: An innocent man is wrongly executed, and his ghost appears to the true murderer night after night until justice is done and his soul can finally rest. The tale is founded on the ancient belief that innocent blood calls for atonement.
moral: Innocent blood cries out for justice, and no guilty party can escape its call.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXV: The Specter in Fjelkinge. Source: Hofberg, p.21. Founded on the ancient belief that innocent blood calls for atonement, and the murdered cannot rest until the deed is brought to light.
